title: 98TH ACADEMY AWARDS (2026) — COMPREHENSIVE CONTEXTUAL GUIDE
ceremony_overview: The 98th Academy Awards ceremony takes place on March 15, 2026, at the Dolby Theatre in Hollywood, Los Angeles. The ceremony is televised in the United States by ABC and streaming on Hulu. Conan O'Brien hosts for the second consecutive year, after receiving acclaim for hosting the previous ceremony. The creative team has described this year's theme as "humanity," with music director Michael Bearden stating, "We are celebrating human touch, human connection, and actual intelligence, not artificial intelligence."

With 16 nominations, **Sinners** is nominated in more categories than any film in Oscar history, surpassing the previous record of 14 shared by *All About Eve* (1950), *Titanic* (1997), and *La La Land* (2016). It also broke the record for most Black individuals nominated for a single film at ten. **One Battle After Another** follows with 13 nominations. **Marty Supreme**, **Frankenstein**, and **Sentimental Value** each earned nine nominations. **Hamnet** received eight.

Best Casting debuts as a competitive category, bringing the total to 24 — the first new Oscar category since Best Animated Feature in 2001. This is also the first ceremony conducted under the Academy's new rule requiring all voting members to view every nominated film in each category before casting a final-round ballot, or else abstain from that category.
Notable names absent from the nominations include Cynthia Erivo and Ariana Grande for *Wicked: For Good*, Adam Sandler for *Jay Kelly*, and George Clooney.

nominees.name: Sinners (Warner Bros.)
details: **Producers:** Zinzi Coogler, Sev Ohanian, Ryan Coogler

A 2025 American horror film written, produced, and directed by Ryan Coogler. Set in 1932 in the Mississippi Delta, the film stars Michael B. Jordan in dual roles as criminal identical twin brothers — Smoke and Stack Moore — who return to their hometown in the Jim Crow South, where they are confronted by a supernatural evil. The ensemble includes Hailee Steinfeld, Miles Caton (in his film debut), Jack O'Connell, Wunmi Mosaku, Jayme Lawson, Omar Benson Miller, and Delroy Lindo.

The film adapts the blood-and-fangs conventions of the vampire genre to explore the folklore and legacy of blues music. The twins' newly opened juke joint is besieged by vampires drawn to a young blues prodigy's talent, reflecting on Black art's cultural appropriation. Deeply personal to Coogler, the film draws on his ancestral history in Mississippi and the story of his maternal grandfather, who migrated to Oakland. Coogler's grief following his uncle's death in 2015 deepened his interest in the blues and its roots in Black history.

Sinners premiered on April 3, 2025, and was theatrically released on April 18. It received universal critical acclaim (97% on Rotten Tomatoes from 429 reviews) and was a commercial smash, grossing over $370 million worldwide against a budget of $90–100 million. It crossed $200 million domestically in its fourth weekend, becoming the first original film to do so since *Coco* in 2017.

The production was delayed from March to April 2025 for additional post-production time due to the scarcity of film stock labs, as the project heavily used film cameras. Director of photography Autumn Durald Arkapaw filmed by combining Ultra Panavision 70 and IMAX formats. In addition to a standard digital release, the film received 10 IMAX 70mm prints and 5 standard 70mm prints.
With 16 nominations, Sinners holds the all-time record for most Oscar nominations by a single film.

nominees.name: One Battle After Another (Warner Bros.)
details: **Producers:** Adam Somner, Sara Murphy, Paul Thomas Anderson

A 2025 American black comedy action-thriller written, produced, and directed by Paul Thomas Anderson, inspired by Thomas Pynchon's 1990 novel *Vineland*. The ensemble is led by Leonardo DiCaprio, Sean Penn, Benicio del Toro, Regina Hall, Teyana Taylor, and Chase Infiniti (in her film debut). The story follows an ex-revolutionary who is forced back into his former combative lifestyle upon being pursued by a corrupt military officer.

Anderson had wanted to adapt *Vineland* since the early 2000s — for around 20 years he tinkered with the script, which at one point was 600 pages long. He eventually incorporated his own stories, and the screenplay is credited as "inspired by" Vineland. He previously adapted Pynchon's *Inherent Vice* (2014). Principal photography took place in California from January to June 2024 using VistaVision, becoming one of the first films to use the format since the 1960s.

With car chases, shootouts, and an action-heavy narrative, the film allegedly cost $150 million — approximately the cost of Anderson's five previous films combined. Initial reactions were overwhelmingly positive, with multiple critics calling it a "masterpiece" (94% on Rotten Tomatoes from 436 reviews). It made $22 million over its opening weekend, topping the box office and marking the best opening for an Anderson-directed film.
13 nominations — the second most-nominated film this year.

nominees.name: Frankenstein (Netflix)
details: **Producers:** Guillermo del Toro, J. Miles Dale, Scott Stuber

A 2025 American Gothic science fiction drama written, produced, and directed by Guillermo del Toro, adapted from Mary Shelley's 1818 novel. Oscar Isaac stars as Victor Frankenstein and Jacob Elordi as the Creature, with Mia Goth, Christoph Waltz, Charles Dance, Felix Kammerer, Lars Mikkelsen, David Bradley, Christian Convery, and Ralph Ineson.

Del Toro developed a version in the early 2000s that was going to be two films. He condensed his earlier idea into two chapters: the first told by Dr. Victor Frankenstein, the second following the Creature. Del Toro emphasized practical craftsmanship: "I want real sets. I don't want digital, I don't want AI, I don't want simulation. I want old-fashioned craftsmanship: people painting, building, hammering, plastering." He doesn't see it as a horror movie: "Ridiculous as it may sound, I see it as a biography of these characters. In my movie, it is about the lineage of familial pain."

Jacob Elordi replaced Andrew Garfield for the role of the Creature due to scheduling conflicts from the SAG-AFTRA strikes. Netflix backed the $120 million production, releasing it in select theaters on October 17, 2025, followed by a global Netflix release on November 7. It holds an 85% on Rotten Tomatoes from 374 reviews (Metacritic: 78). Variety reported it would receive a Criterion Collection physical home media release.
Nine nominations, tying with Marty Supreme and Sentimental Value.

nominees.name: Hamnet (Focus Features)
details: **Producers:** Liza Marshall, Pippa Harris, Nicolas Gonda, Steven Spielberg, Sam Mendes

A 2025 historical drama directed by Chloé Zhao, who co-wrote the screenplay with Maggie O'Farrell, based on O'Farrell's bestselling 2020 novel. The film dramatizes the family life of William Shakespeare and his wife Agnes (Anne Hathaway) as they cope with the death of their 11-year-old son Hamnet. Jessie Buckley and Paul Mescal star as Agnes and William, with Emily Watson, Joe Alwyn, Noah Jupe, Jacobi Jupe (as Hamnet), and Olivia Lynes.

Cinematographer Łukasz Żal (*Ida*, *Cold War*) mostly exploits static shots with carefully composed mise-en-scènes. Composer Max Richter provides the score; the final ten minutes feature Richter's "On The Nature of Daylight," creating a powerful sequence of personified grief. Zhao described the film as an attempt to balance masculine and feminine forces. Buckley delivers what critics called a heroic performance, while Mescal plays Shakespeare pouring his grief into "the Danish play."

Hamnet premiered at the 52nd Telluride Film Festival on August 29, 2025, where it won the prestigious People's Choice Award at TIFF, making Zhao only the second director to win that prize twice. It won Best Motion Picture – Drama and Best Actress for Buckley at the 83rd Golden Globe Awards. Limited release began November 26, with wide release on December 5. Budget: $30 million. Worldwide gross: $89.1 million. Rotten Tomatoes: 87% from 336 reviews (Metacritic: 84).
Eight nominations total.

nominees.name: Marty Supreme (A24)
details: **Producers:** Eli Bush, Ronald Bronstein, Josh Safdie, Anthony Katagas, Timothée Chalamet

A 2025 American sports comedy-drama directed by Josh Safdie, who co-wrote it with Ronald Bronstein. Set in the 1950s, Timothée Chalamet stars as Marty Mauser, a table tennis player loosely based on real-life champion and hustler Marty Reisman. In 1952 New York City, Mauser works as a shoe salesman at his uncle Murray's shop while competing professionally, leading high-stakes hustles and schemes to reach a major tournament in Tokyo.

The project originated in 2018 when Safdie's wife and executive producer Sara Rossein bought a copy of Reisman's 1974 autobiography *The Money Player*. Safdie presented the project to Chalamet by focusing on Reisman's physical similarities to the actor; Chalamet accepted and began taking table tennis lessons that same year. Safdie's interest in table tennis began in his youth, as his grandparents often had "eccentric Jewish immigrant Lower East Side characters" playing the sport at their house. This is Safdie's first solo directorial effort since *The Pleasure of Being Robbed* (2008).

The cast features over 100 roles, mixing professional actors with non-professionals — a Safdie trademark. Beyond Chalamet, the film stars Gwyneth Paltrow, Odessa A'zion, and Larry "Ratso" Sloman, alongside non-actor personalities including Tyler, The Creator; NBA Hall of Famer George Gervin; director Abel Ferrara; Fran Drescher; Sandra Bernhard; and Philippe Petit.

Cinematographer Darius Khondji shot on 35mm film stock using Arriflex cameras and vintage Panavision C Series and B Series anamorphic lenses to recreate the feel of the 1950s. Electronic artist Daniel Lopatin composed the score, working for around 10 weeks at a Manhattan studio, with mixing at Electric Lady Studios. Musician Weyes Blood provided background vocals. Principal photography ran in New York City from September to December 2024, with additional filming in Japan in February 2025.

The film premiered at the New York Film Festival on October 6, 2025, and was released on December 25 by A24. It received critical acclaim and has grossed $179.1 million worldwide, becoming A24's highest-grossing film. Chalamet won a Golden Globe and Critics' Choice Award for his performance. To promote the film, Chalamet directed a viral mock-Zoom video satirizing corporate marketing culture. A24 also flew an orange blimp emblazoned with "Marty Supreme" across the United States. Runtime: 149 minutes.
Nine nominations, tying with Frankenstein and Sentimental Value.

nominees.name: Sentimental Value (Neon)
details: **Producers:** Maria Ekerhovd, Andrea Berentsen Ottmar

A 2025 Norwegian drama directed by Joachim Trier, co-written with longtime collaborator Eskil Vogt. Winner of the Grand Prix at the 2025 Cannes Film Festival, where it received a 19-minute standing ovation after its premiere on May 21. Runtime: 135 minutes.

Sisters Nora and Agnes reunite with their estranged father Gustav, a charismatic former filmmaker hoping to stage a comeback. Stage actress Nora (Renate Reinsve) is offered the lead role in Gustav's new film, but when she turns it down, she discovers he has given the part to Rachel Kemp (Elle Fanning), an eager young Hollywood star so moved by one of Gustav's old films that she invites him to dinner. Trier plays with Bergman-esque themes of identity — there's even a direct visual allusion to *Persona* — especially when Rachel dyes her hair to look like the director's daughter. The film also addresses generational trauma: Gustav's mother Karin was arrested and tortured by the Nazis, and the agony she carried always stayed with her.

Stellan Skarsgård plays Gustav, Renate Reinsve plays Nora, Inga Ibsdotter Lilleaas plays Agnes, Elle Fanning plays Rachel, and Anders Danielsen Lie and Cory Michael Smith appear in smaller roles. Danish cinematographer Kasper Tuxen shot mainly on 35mm, with flashback scenes set in the 1920s–30s shot in 16mm. Principal photography commenced in August 2024 in Oslo, shooting 63 days until late November, with key scenes also filmed on location in Deauville, France.

On Rotten Tomatoes, 96% of 213 reviews are positive (Metacritic: 86, "universal acclaim"). Nominated for eight Golden Globes, winning Best Supporting Actor for Skarsgård. Remarkably, four of the film's actors received Oscar acting nominations — a rare ensemble achievement.
Nine nominations — the most for any Norwegian film in Oscar history.

nominees.name: Bugonia (Focus Features)
details: **Producers:** Ed Guiney & Andrew Lowe, Yorgos Lanthimos, Emma Stone, Lars Knudsen

A 2025 English-language remake of the 2003 South Korean film *Save the Green Planet!* by Jang Joon-hwan, directed by Yorgos Lanthimos with a screenplay by Will Tracy. A co-production of the UK, Ireland, South Korea, and the United States.

The sweaty, obsessive Teddy (Jesse Plemons) teams with his impressionable cousin Donny (Aidan Delbis) to kidnap CEO Michelle Fuller (Emma Stone), believing she's an alien from the Andromeda species intent on destroying humanity. The cast also includes Stavros Halkias and Alicia Silverstone.

The title refers to a folk ritual described in ancient Mediterranean texts, including Virgil's *Georgics*, involving the sacrifice of a cow and the belief that bees would spontaneously generate from its carcass. Development began in 2020 with Jang attached to direct and Tracy adapting the screenplay. Jang stepped down citing health concerns but remained an executive producer; by February 2024, Lanthimos was hired, and Stone joined as actress and producer.

Irish cinematographer Robbie Ryan shot on 8-perf 35mm film with VistaVision cameras — approximately 95% of the film, by Ryan's estimate, using the format more than any film since *One-Eyed Jacks* (1961). With a budget of $45–55 million, Bugonia is Lanthimos's most expensive film. Lanthimos wanted the ending filmed at the Acropolis of Athens, but Greece's Central Archaeological Council rejected the request; Sarakiniko Beach on Milos was chosen instead.
Bugonia premiered in the main competition at the 82nd Venice International Film Festival on August 28, 2025. Worldwide gross: $43.3 million. Rotten Tomatoes: 87% from 331 reviews.

nominees.name: F1 (Apple Original Films / Warner Bros.)
details: **Producers:** Chad Oman, Brad Pitt, Dede Gardner, Jeremy Kleiner, Joseph Kosinski, Jerry Bruckheimer

A 2025 American sports drama starring Brad Pitt as Sonny Hayes, a Formula 1 driver who returns from a 30-year retirement to save his former teammate's underdog team, APXGP, from collapse. Directed by Joseph Kosinski (*Top Gun: Maverick*). Also stars Javier Bardem, Damson Idris, Kerry Condon, and Tobias Menzies. F1 driver Lewis Hamilton served as both producer and actor.

Filming on real F1 racing courses with actors actually driving at up to 180 mph gave the film a profound sense of realism. VFX supervisor Ryan Tudhope leveraged techniques from *Top Gun: Maverick* to re-skin jets, here replacing real Grand Prix cars with fictional APXGP cars. Director of photography: Claudio Miranda. Editor: Stephen Mirrione, ACE (Oscar winner for *Traffic*). Composer: Hans Zimmer. Production designers: Mark Tildesley and Ben Munro. Costume designer: Julian Day. Casting director: Lucy Bevan.

Pitt was paid $30 million; the budget has been reported between $200–300 million. Released on June 27, 2025, the film surpassed $629 million globally, becoming the highest-grossing sports film of all time and 2025's highest-grossing original feature. Rotten Tomatoes: 82% from 358 reviews. Runtime: 2 hours 35 minutes.

nominees.name: The Secret Agent (Neon)
details: **Producer:** Emilie Lesclaux

*O Agente Secreto* is a 2025 Brazilian historical political thriller written and directed by Kleber Mendonça Filho (*Aquarius*, *Bacurau*). Wagner Moura stars as Armando, a former professor caught in political turmoil during the Brazilian military dictatorship, attempting to flee persecution and resist an authoritarian regime. The supporting cast includes Carlos Francisco, Tânia Maria, Gabriel Leone, Maria Fernanda Cândido, Alice Carvalho, and Udo Kier in his final film role before his death in November 2025.

The film is a neo-noir historical political thriller, heavily inspired by Robert Altman, Brian De Palma, Sam Peckinpah, Martin Scorsese, and Steven Spielberg productions of the 1970s. Principal photography took place over ten weeks in 2024, on location in Recife, Pernambuco, with many scenes filmed in historical buildings. It marks Moura's first Portuguese-language production in eight years.

World premiere at the 2025 Cannes Film Festival on May 18, where it received a 13-minute standing ovation and became the festival's most awarded film: Best Actor for Moura (the first Brazilian to win the prize), Best Director for Mendonça Filho (the second Brazilian filmmaker to do so), plus the Art House Cinema Award and the FIPRESCI Prize. At the 83rd Golden Globe Awards, it became the first Brazilian film nominated for Best Motion Picture – Drama.

Despite Mendonça Filho having been previously passed over twice by Brazil's Oscar submission commission — for *Aquarius* in 2016 (due to political boycott under the Temer administration) and *Bacurau* in 2019 (during the Bolsonaro administration) — *The Secret Agent* was selected as the country's submission. Runtime: 2 hours 41 minutes.

nominees.name: Train Dreams (Netflix)
details: **Producers:** Marissa McMahon, Teddy Schwarzman, Will Janowitz, Ashley Schlaifer, Michael Heimler

A 2025 American period drama directed by Clint Bentley, co-written with Greg Kwedar, based on Denis Johnson's acclaimed 2002 novella (a finalist for the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction). Stars Joel Edgerton, Felicity Jones, Nathaniel Arcand, Clifton Collins Jr., John Diehl, Paul Schneider, and Kerry Condon. Will Patton provided the voiceover — he had also previously narrated the audiobook of Johnson's novella.

Bentley cited *Andrei Rublev*, *Princess Mononoke*, *Days of Heaven*, *Jules et Jim*, and *Y Tu Mamá También* as influences. The movie was shot entirely in natural light using an ARRI Alexa 35 camera by cinematographer Adolpho Veloso, who built a customized candle rig to work around the set's no-open-flames rule. Principal photography started in Washington in April 2024, filming in Tekoa, Snoqualmie, Spokane, Metaline Falls, and Colville. The score is by Bryce Dessner (of The National).

In Bentley's second feature (following *Jockey*, 2021; he also co-wrote *Sing Sing*, 2024), the film received a beautiful reception at Sundance and has continued to grow in stature. Rotten Tomatoes: 94% from 236 reviews (Metacritic: 88, "universal acclaim").

nominees.name: Paul Thomas Anderson — *One Battle After Another*
details: Widely considered one of the greatest American filmmakers of his generation. Filmography includes *Boogie Nights*, *Magnolia*, *There Will Be Blood*, *The Master*, *Phantom Thread*, and *Licorice Pizza*. He has been nominated for Best Director multiple times but has never won. He has two previous Best Original Screenplay nominations (*Boogie Nights*, *Magnolia*).

nominees.name: Ryan Coogler — *Sinners*
details: Previous films include *Fruitvale Station*, *Creed*, and *Black Panther* (the first superhero film nominated for Best Picture). Sinners represents his fifth collaboration with Michael B. Jordan. The film's deeply personal foundation draws on Coogler's family ties to Mississippi and the Great Migration.

nominees.name: Josh Safdie — *Marty Supreme*
details: Formerly of the Safdie brothers directing team (*Good Time*, *Uncut Gems*), directing solo for the first time since *The Pleasure of Being Robbed* (2008). This is his first Academy Award nomination for directing. His brother Benny Safdie is also represented in this year's race, having directed *The Smashing Machine*.

nominees.name: Joachim Trier — *Sentimental Value*
details: Norwegian director known for *Reprise*, *Oslo, August 31st*, and *The Worst Person in the World* (which earned Renate Reinsve the Best Actress prize at Cannes 2021). Co-written with frequent collaborator Eskil Vogt. This is his first Oscar nomination.

nominees.name: Chloé Zhao — *Hamnet*
details: Zhao won Best Director for *Nomadland* (2021), becoming the second woman and first woman of color to win in that category. With this nomination, she becomes only the second woman to earn multiple Best Director nominations. She co-wrote the adapted screenplay with novelist Maggie O'Farrell.

nominees.name: Timothée Chalamet — *Marty Supreme*
details: A Best Actor nominee for *Call Me by Your Name* (2017) at just 22. He has since starred in *Little Women*, the *Dune* franchise, and *Wonka*. In *Marty Supreme*, he plays Marty Mauser, a table tennis prodigy-turned-hustler. He also serves as a producer. He won a Golden Globe and Critics' Choice Award for the performance. He cited *The Hustler* and *The Color of Money* as references for the role.

nominees.name: Leonardo DiCaprio — *One Battle After Another*
details: This is only his fourth performance since 2019's *Once Upon a Time in Hollywood*. He won his first Oscar for *The Revenant* (2016). He reportedly received his standard $25 million fee. He plays Bob Ferguson, a former revolutionary living off the grid with his daughter.

nominees.name: Ethan Hawke — *Blue Moon*
details: Hawke has been nominated four times previously (twice for acting, twice for writing). His long collaboration with Richard Linklater spans *Boyhood*, the *Before* trilogy, and more. Linklater felt Hawke was not old enough for the role until recently and worked on the film for 12 years. The performance has been widely called career-best work. *Blue Moon* tells the story of lyricist Lorenz Hart's struggles with alcoholism and mental health on the night of March 31, 1943, as "Oklahoma!" opens. It is the only film nominated in any major category identifiable as an LGBTQ film.

nominees.name: Michael B. Jordan — *Sinners*
details: Jordan delivers a dual performance as Smoke and Stack Moore — twin brothers and World War I veterans turned bootleggers. This is his first Oscar nomination and his fifth collaboration with Coogler. Gold Derby gives him a 67% chance of winning.

nominees.name: Wagner Moura — *The Secret Agent*
details: Brazilian actor best known internationally for portraying Pablo Escobar in *Narcos*. This marks his first Oscar nod. He became the first Brazilian actor to win the Cannes Best Actor prize for this role.

nominees.name: Jessie Buckley — *Hamnet*
details: Known for *Wild Rose*, *I'm Thinking of Ending Things*, *The Lost Daughter* (for which she was Oscar-nominated for Supporting Actress), and *Women Talking*. She leads Best Actress predictions at 96.8% in Awards Daily polls. She won Best Actress at the Golden Globes for this performance.

nominees.name: Rose Byrne — *If I Had Legs I'd Kick You*
details: A 2025 American drama written and directed by Mary Bronstein, released by A24. Byrne plays Linda, a psychotherapist stretched to her limits while caring for her daughter, who has a pediatric feeding disorder. Also stars Conan O'Brien, Christian Slater, and A$AP Rocky. Described as "Uncut Gems for moms." Bronstein is the partner of Ronald Bronstein (co-writer of *Marty Supreme*), and the film was produced by the Safdie/Bronstein circle (Sara Murphy, Ronald Bronstein, Josh Safdie, Eli Bush).

Byrne won the Silver Bear for Best Leading Performance at the 75th Berlin International Film Festival, an Independent Spirit Award, and a Golden Globe. This is her first Oscar nomination. Rotten Tomatoes: 92% from 190 reviews. The film premiered at Sundance on January 24, 2025.

nominees.name: Kate Hudson — *Song Sung Blue*
details: A musical drama directed by Craig Brewer, based on the true story of Mike and Claire Sardina, who performed as Neil Diamond tribute band Lightning & Thunder. Hugh Jackman and Kate Hudson star. Neil Diamond granted the rights to use his music. Hudson is really singing in the film. One year before release, Hudson launched her music career with her debut studio album *Glorious* (2024) — Jackman saw her discussing music in an interview and realized she'd be the perfect co-star.

Hudson was previously nominated for Best Supporting Actress for *Almost Famous* (2000), marking her return to Oscar contention after over two decades. The film premiered at AFI Fest on October 26, 2025, and was released on December 25 by Focus Features. Gross: $58 million. Rotten Tomatoes: 77% from 204 reviews.

nominees.name: Renate Reinsve — *Sentimental Value*
details: Norwegian actress who won the Best Actress award at Cannes for *The Worst Person in the World* (2021), also directed by Joachim Trier. This is their reunion. She plays Nora, an accomplished stage actor grieving her mother's death while dealing with the return of her estranged father. This is her first Oscar nomination.

nominees.name: Emma Stone — *Bugonia*
details: Stone won Best Actress for *La La Land* (2017) and *Poor Things* (2024). She also serves as a producer on *Bugonia*, continuing her ongoing collaboration with Lanthimos (their fourth together after *The Favourite*, *Poor Things*, and *Kinds of Kindness*). This marks her fourth Oscar nomination.

nominees.name: Benicio del Toro — *One Battle After Another*
details: Won Best Supporting Actor for *Traffic* (2001). He plays Sensei, the unfettered head of a migrant underground railroad. He joined the cast during a break in production. This is his third Oscar nomination.

nominees.name: Jacob Elordi — *Frankenstein*
details: Known for *Euphoria* and *Saltburn*. He plays the Creature opposite Oscar Isaac's Victor Frankenstein. This is his first Oscar nomination. He replaced Andrew Garfield in the role due to scheduling conflicts from the SAG-AFTRA strikes.

nominees.name: Delroy Lindo — *Sinners*
details: Earning his first Oscar nomination after over 50 years of acting. The veteran actor is known for *Malcolm X*, *Crooklyn*, *Get Shorty*, *Clockers*, and Spike Lee's *Da 5 Bloods*. His nomination was considered a surprise.

nominees.name: Sean Penn — *One Battle After Another*
details: Penn won Best Actor Oscars for *Mystic River* (2004) and *Milk* (2009). He plays Colonel Lockjaw, the film's antagonist. He is the frontrunner in this category, with a 67% chance of winning his third Oscar, per Gold Derby.

nominees.name: Stellan Skarsgård — *Sentimental Value*
details: The Swedish veteran actor is known for *Good Will Hunting*, *Chernobyl*, the *Thor* franchise, and *Dune*. He plays Gustav, the estranged father and once-renowned filmmaker. This is his first Oscar nomination. He won Best Supporting Actor at the 83rd Golden Globe Awards for this performance.

nominees.name: Elle Fanning — *Sentimental Value*
details: At 27, Fanning earns her first Academy Award nomination for her role as Rachel Kemp, a young American actress who becomes entangled in a fraught emotional triangle with Gustav Borg and his daughter Nora. Her career spans work with auteurs including Alejandro González Iñárritu, David Fincher, and Sofia Coppola, and credits including *Super 8*, *Maleficent*, *The Beguiled*, *The Neon Demon*, and *A Complete Unknown*. She earned an Emmy nomination for Hulu's *The Great*. She and sister Dakota are co-producing *The Nightingale*, set for 2027 release.

nominees.name: Inga Ibsdotter Lilleaas — *Sentimental Value*
details: Born April 9, 1989, raised in Gol Municipality, Norway. Her parents ran a costume and equipment rental company for amateur stage productions. She lived as a foreign exchange student in Brazil, studied psychology, trained at the Lee Strasberg Theatre and Film Institute in New York, and graduated from Nord University (specializing in physical theatre). She also worked as a dental hygienist, drama teacher, and elderly caregiver.

She plays Agnes, Nora's younger sister. She won the National Board of Review Award for Best Supporting Actress and earned Golden Globe and BAFTA nominations. The role catapulted the 36-year-old Oslo-based actress internationally, including signing with CAA. This is her first Oscar nomination and international breakthrough. On the now-famous improvised hug scene, she recalled director Trier sensing what she was feeling and telling her: "Go hug her."

nominees.name: Amy Madigan — *Weapons*
details: Won the supporting actress award at the SAG Awards earlier in 2026. Known for *Field of Dreams* and *Twice in a Lifetime* (for which she was previously Oscar-nominated). She plays Gladys, described as a "twisted, witchy aunt who has a lock on an entire small town's young children." Critics called her work "award-caliber" and "sublime." *Weapons*, directed by Zach Cregger (*Barbarian*), grossed over $268 million worldwide. Rotten Tomatoes: 93% from 393 reviews.

nominees.name: Wunmi Mosaku — *Sinners*
details: British-Nigerian actress known for *His House*, *Lovecraft Country*, and *Loki*. She plays Annie, Smoke's true love and the town's trusted spiritual healer and Hoodoo conjurer. This is her first Oscar nomination.

nominees.name: Teyana Taylor — *One Battle After Another*
details: Won a Golden Globe for her performance, calling the Oscar nomination "beyond anything I ever allowed myself to fully believe was possible." Known primarily as a singer, dancer, and choreographer, Taylor has expanded into acting. Named in The Hollywood Reporter's "25 Best Movie Performances of 2025" list. This is her first Oscar nomination.

nominees.name: Bugonia — Will Tracy
details: Tracy adapted the screenplay from the 2003 South Korean film *Save the Green Planet!*. He is known for writing the *Succession* episode "Connor's Wedding" and the screenplay for *The Menu*.

nominees.name: Frankenstein — Guillermo del Toro
details: Del Toro adapted Mary Shelley's 1818 novel, structuring his screenplay in two chapters: the first told by Victor Frankenstein, the second following the Creature. He previously won Best Director and Best Picture for *The Shape of Water* (2018) and Best Animated Feature for *Pinocchio* (2022).

nominees.name: Hamnet — Chloé Zhao and Maggie O'Farrell
details: Adapted from O'Farrell's bestselling 2020 novel about the life and death of Shakespeare's son. O'Farrell co-wrote the screenplay with director Zhao.

nominees.name: One Battle After Another — Paul Thomas Anderson
details: Inspired by *Vineland* by Thomas Pynchon. Anderson constructed the script from three previous movie ideas. He has two previous Best Original Screenplay nominations (*Boogie Nights*, *Magnolia*).

nominees.name: Train Dreams — Clint Bentley and Greg Kwedar
details: Adapted from Denis Johnson's 2002 novella, a Pulitzer Prize finalist. Bentley also directed; he and Kwedar previously collaborated on *Jockey* (2021) and *Sing Sing* (2024).

nominees.name: Blue Moon — Robert Kaplow
details: A Richard Linklater–directed biopic about lyricist Lorenz Hart, confined to one set and unfolding in near-real time on the night of March 31, 1943. Premiered at the 75th Berlin International Film Festival on February 18, 2025. Rotten Tomatoes: 90% from 199 reviews. Runtime: 100 minutes. Andrew Scott won the Silver Bear for Best Supporting Performance at the Berlinale. Stars Ethan Hawke, Margaret Qualley, Bobby Cannavale, and Andrew Scott. It is the only major-category film identifiable as an LGBTQ film this year.

nominees.name: It Was Just an Accident — Jafar Panahi (with Nader Saïvar, Shadmehr Rastin, Mehdi Mahmoudian)
details: A thriller about a group of former Iranian political prisoners debating whether to exact revenge on a man they believe may be their former tormentor. Panahi filmed in secret without official permission. The idea came from his prison experience between July 2022 and February 2023. Actresses in the film do not always wear the hijab, which is compulsory under Iranian law. Winner of the Palme d'Or at the 2025 Cannes Film Festival. Rotten Tomatoes: 98% from 210 reviews (Metacritic: 91). Martin Scorsese praised the film: "I have never seen anything quite like it."

nominees.name: Marty Supreme — Ronald Bronstein & Josh Safdie
details: The Safdie signature style of high-energy, anxiety-ridden storytelling applied to the world of competitive table tennis hustling. Bronstein also co-edited the film.

nominees.name: Sentimental Value — Eskil Vogt & Joachim Trier
details: The Norwegian co-writing duo previously collaborated on *The Worst Person in the World*, *Reprise*, and *Oslo, August 31st*. Vogt is also an acclaimed director in his own right (*The Innocents*).

nominees.name: Sinners — Ryan Coogler
details: Coogler won Best Original Screenplay at the 79th BAFTA Awards. He drew heavily from the history of the Jim Crow-era Mississippi Delta and the folklore and legacy of the blues.

nominees.name: Bugonia — Jerskin Fendrix
details: British experimental musician Fendrix previously scored Lanthimos's *Poor Things*, earning a BAFTA nomination.

nominees.name: Frankenstein — Alexandre Desplat
details: Two-time Oscar winner (*The Grand Budapest Hotel*, *The Shape of Water*) and one of the most nominated composers in recent Oscar history. Previously worked with del Toro on *The Shape of Water* and *Pinocchio*. For this score, Desplat suggested going the opposite of the Creature's extreme strength, finding "the smallest, most fragile, beautiful and precious instrument, which is the violin."

nominees.name: Hamnet — Max Richter
details: Celebrated neo-classical composer known for *The Leftovers*, *Ad Astra*, and concert works. The final ten minutes of *Hamnet* feature his iconic "On The Nature of Daylight," creating a powerful sequence of personified grief.

nominees.name: One Battle After Another — Jonny Greenwood
details: Frequent Anderson collaborator and lead keyboardist of Radiohead. Employs eclectic instruments including xylophone and marimba. Oscar-nominated multiple times for his work with Anderson (*There Will Be Blood*, *Phantom Thread*, *The Power of the Dog*).

nominees.name: Sinners — Ludwig Göransson
details: Longtime Coogler collaborator who also served as an executive producer. He pulled extensively from the lineage of Mississippi Delta blues, drawing from old records Coogler shared with him, including those of Robert Johnson and Tommy Johnson. Göransson won the Oscar for *Black Panther* (2019).

nominees.name: "Dear Me" — *Diane Warren: Relentless*
details: **Songwriter:** Diane Warren. **Performed by:** Kesha.

This is Warren's 17th Oscar nomination — a record for the most nominations without a win. This is also the ninth year in a row she has been nominated, setting a new record for the most consecutive nominations in the history of the category. She has earned a Grammy, an Emmy, and an honorary Academy Award. The song is a letter to Warren's younger self, inspired by her childhood struggles. Warren described the inspiration: writing to "that young girl that sat and felt like shit and felt so alone and the kids were super mean to." The documentary is directed by Bess Kargman and features interviews with Cher, Clive Davis, Gloria Estefan, Jennifer Hudson, Jerry Bruckheimer, and others. Warren — who is "proudly on the spectrum" — also revealed she was sexually molested as a child.

nominees.name: "Golden" — *KPop Demon Hunters*
details: **Songwriters:** EJAE, Mark Sonnenblick, Joong Gyu Kwak, Yu Han Lee, Hee Dong Nam, Jeong Hoon Seo, and Teddy Park. **Performed by:** EJAE, Audrey Nuna & Rei Ami.

"Golden" broke Official Charts records in 2025, claiming ten weeks at Number 1. It won Best Original Song at both the Golden Globes and Critics' Choice Awards. Considered the closest thing to a sure bet at this year's Oscars. The song needed to be good enough that, both in the real world and within the film, it would become an instant phenomenon — and it succeeded on both counts.

nominees.name: "I Lied to You" — *Sinners*
details: **Songwriters:** Raphael Saadiq and Ludwig Göransson. **Performed by:** Miles Caton.

The song scores what critics have called the best scene of 2025: Miles Caton's "Sammie" Moore performs at the juke joint, taking audiences on a journey through the past, present, and future of music. In just over five minutes, the blues song slips into funk, references hip-hop, tribal music, and more — an exceptional experience of music history. The Oscar telecast performance will feature Caton and Saadiq joined by Misty Copeland, Eric Gales, Brittany Howard, Christone "Kingfish" Ingram, Bobby Rush, Shaboozey, and Alice Smith.

nominees.name: "Sweet Dreams of Joy" — *Viva Verdi!*
details: **Songwriter:** Nicholas Pike. **Performed by:** soprano Ana María Martínez.

From a documentary about Casa Verdi, a Milan retirement home built by Giuseppe Verdi in 1896 for retired musicians. Directed by Yvonne Russo and produced by Christine La Monte and the late Ron Simons (his final producing credit). Pike is an Emmy-winning composer; the aria is one of the rarest operatic compositions to break through in the Best Original Song category. The song was originally written in 2017 but is eligible because it was composed for the film, which took years to reach release. Collider called the nomination "the biggest head-scratcher of the Oscar nominations."

nominees.name: "Train Dreams" — *Train Dreams*
details: **Music:** Nick Cave and Bryce Dessner. **Lyric:** Nick Cave.

This is the first Oscar nomination for both Cave and Dessner. The song plays over the closing credits rather than being incorporated into the film itself, but it directly expounds on the film's ideas — a life lived, dark visions of death, an accumulation of traumas and wonders. Critics called it lyrically the most powerful of all the nominees, with Dessner's sparse piano instrumentation letting Cave's words soar.

nominees.name: It Was Just an Accident (Iran)
details: Directed by Jafar Panahi. Winner of the Palme d'Or at Cannes 2025. Panahi has won top prizes at Cannes, Berlin, and Venice. He has faced imprisonment and travel bans from the Iranian government. This is the first Iranian film nominated for Golden Globe Best Motion Picture – Drama, Best Director, and Best Screenplay. Also nominated for Best Original Screenplay. Following the Cannes win, French foreign minister Jean-Noël Barrot called Panahi's win "a gesture of resistance against the Iranian regime's oppression," provoking a diplomatic incident. Co-production of Iran, France, and Luxembourg. Rotten Tomatoes: 98% (Metacritic: 91).

nominees.name: Sentimental Value (Norway)
details: Directed by Joachim Trier. Winner of the Grand Prix at Cannes 2025. Nine Oscar nominations — the most for any Norwegian film. Also nominated for Best Picture, Best Director, Best Original Screenplay, and four acting categories.

nominees.name: Sirât (Spain)
details: Directed by Oliver Laxe, co-written with Santiago Fillol. A father (Sergi López) searches for his missing daughter along with his son and a group of ravers in the deserts of southern Morocco. Inspired by the Sirāt Bridge, which in Islamic tradition separates hell and heaven. Winner of the Jury Prize at Cannes 2025. Shot by Mauro Herce on Super 16mm film. Score by electronic musician Kangding Ray. Most of the cast are non-professionals selected through street casting. Produced by Pedro and Agustín Almodóvar. Polled as the best film in the 2025 Cannes Critics Survey by IndieWire. Eleven Goya Award nominations. Metacritic: 85. Runtime: 115 minutes. Also nominated for Best Sound.

nominees.name: The Secret Agent (Brazil)
details: Directed by Kleber Mendonça Filho. The most awarded film at Cannes 2025. Also nominated for Best Picture, Best Actor, and Best Casting. Wagner Moura's first Portuguese-language production in eight years. Features Udo Kier's final film role.

nominees.name: The Voice of Hind Rajab (Tunisia)
details: Directed by Kaouther Ben Hania, who uses the real voice recordings of five-year-old Palestinian Hind Rajab's desperate phone call for help, made while trapped in a car in Gaza City. Hind and several relatives were killed by Israeli forces on January 29, 2024. Actors re-enact the Palestinian Red Crescent staff's desperate rescue attempts. The film is set entirely inside Red Crescent offices and uses an all-Palestinian cast.

Won the Grand Jury Prize Silver Lion at the Venice International Film Festival, where it received a 23-minute standing ovation. This is Ben Hania's third consecutive Oscar nomination (after *The Man Who Sold His Skin* and *Four Daughters*), making her the first Arab woman to direct three Oscar-nominated films. One of the film's stars, Motaz Malhees, cannot attend the ceremony due to Trump's travel ban on Palestine. Rajab's mother, Wissam Hamada, will watch from Greece, where her family was granted asylum. If Ben Hania wins, she will become the first Arab woman director to receive an Academy Award. Runtime: 89 minutes.

nominees.name: Arco (Neon)
details: Directed by Ugo Bienvenu in his feature debut, co-written with Félix de Givry. Produced by Oscar-winner Natalie Portman. Set in the year 2932, where humanity has learned to time-travel. Ten-year-old Arco steals his sister's time-traveling cape and crashes into the year 2075, where he befriends a girl named Iris. Bienvenu developed the film visually from his own sketches rather than a script. He used 10–20 drawings as the skeleton and spent three years on storyboards; de Givry translated the images into a script. They self-financed and hired three people to create a full-length animatic to secure investors.

English dub features Natalie Portman, Mark Ruffalo, Will Ferrell, and Andy Samberg. Premiered at Cannes on May 16, 2025. Won the top prize at Annecy. Acquired by Neon. Compared to the work of Hayao Miyazaki for its wonder-filled aesthetic and ability to weave whimsy with ecological meditation. Hand-drawn animation that balances nostalgia with cutting-edge design. Rotten Tomatoes: 92% from 71 reviews. Runtime: 88 minutes. US box office under $1 million — the smallest-grossing animated feature nominee.

nominees.name: Elio (Pixar/Disney)
details: Directed by Madeline Sharafian, Domee Shi, and Adrian Molina. Stars the voices of Yonas Kibreab, Zoe Saldaña, Brad Garrett, and Jameela Jamil. An eleven-year-old boy named Elio Solís is mistaken for Earth's intergalactic ambassador after being beamed up by aliens. Conceived by Molina as a story about childhood and social isolation, inspired by growing up on a military base.

The film had a troubled production history. Molina left due to creative differences; Shi and Sharafian replaced him. Pixar CCO Pete Docter admitted to ordering the removal of gay content, causing significant controversy. Premiered June 10, 2025; released June 20 — the same day KPop Demon Hunters hit Netflix. Had the lowest opening weekend in Pixar history. Grossed $154 million worldwide against a $150–200 million budget, underperforming. Rotten Tomatoes: 83% from 229 reviews.

nominees.name: KPop Demon Hunters (Sony Pictures Animation/Netflix)
details: Co-written and directed by Maggie Kang and Chris Appelhans. A K-pop girl group, Huntrix, leads double lives as demon hunters, facing off against a rival boy band whose members are secretly demons. The concept was inspired by Korean shamans (mudang), who historically sang and danced to protect their communities.

A massive cultural phenomenon: most-watched original title in Netflix history with over 500 million views. First film soundtrack to have four songs simultaneously in the Billboard Hot 100 top ten; double platinum in the US. Google's top five most-searched Halloween costumes were all characters from the film. Won Best Animated Feature and Best Original Song at both the Critics' Choice Awards and Golden Globes. Rotten Tomatoes: 97%. Budget: approximately $100 million. A Criterion Collection physical release was announced. Sequel confirmed for 2029. Also nominated for Best Original Song ("Golden").

nominees.name: Little Amélie or the Character of Rain (Maybe Movies/Ikki Films)
details: Directed by Maïlys Vallade and Liane-Cho Han. Adaptation of Amélie Nothomb's novel *The Character of Rain* (*Métaphysique des tubes*). Amélie is born in a vegetative state to a Belgian family living in Kobe, Japan, and believes her condition makes her a god. On her second birthday, an earthquake causes her to break free.

Han discovered Nothomb's novel at age 19. Years later, while working with Vallade on *Calamity, a Childhood of Martha Jane Cannary*, he gave her a copy, planting the seed. The team finished in just 14 months — extraordinarily fast for European feature animation. Won the Audience Award at Annecy. North American premiere at TIFF. Distributed in the US by GKIDS. Rotten Tomatoes: 98% from 56 reviews — the highest-rated animated feature nominee. Runtime: 77 minutes — the shortest.

nominees.name: Zootopia 2 (Walt Disney Animation Studios)
details: Written and directed by Jared Bush; co-directed by Byron Howard; produced by Yvett Merino. Stars Ginnifer Goodwin, Jason Bateman, and Ke Huy Quan as a venomous snake whose arrival in the animal metropolis drives the central mystery.

Record-breaking $559.5 million global opening over Thanksgiving. Passed *A Minecraft Movie* to rank as the highest-grossing domestic release of 2025 with $424.2 million. Worldwide gross: $1.864 billion — the second-highest-grossing animated film of all time, ninth-highest-grossing film of all time. First Hollywood film in recent memory to do mega-business in China, earning over $650 million to become the top-grossing Western film of all time there, surpassing *Avengers: Endgame*.

The original *Zootopia* won the Animated Feature Oscar in 2017. Unused recordings of Tommy Lister Jr. from the first film were utilized for Finnick in the sequel; Lister died in 2020, and the film is dedicated to his memory. Rotten Tomatoes: 91%.

nominees.name: Come See Me in the Good Light (Apple TV+)
details: Directed by Ryan White. Retraces the life of American poet and activist Andrea Gibson, from childhood struggles with identity and trauma, through their work as a voice for the LGBTQ community, to their battle with terminal ovarian cancer diagnosed in 2021. In 2023, Gibson was named Colorado's poet laureate.

Conceived by comedian Tig Notaro and producer Stef Willen. Abby Wambach and Glennon Doyle contributed half a million dollars within minutes. Sara Bareilles and Brandi Carlile wrote an original song for the film. As White recalled: "Tig called us a couple years ago with this idea. We said truly what could be a less funny combination of words than poetry and cancer?"

White noted that the assumption was always that Gibson would die before the film came out — but Gibson was still alive at the Sundance premiere. Premiered at Sundance on January 25, 2025, winning the Festival Favorite Award. Released on Apple TV+ on November 14, 2025. Rotten Tomatoes: 100% from 51 reviews.

nominees.name: Cutting Through Rocks
details: Directed by Mohammadreza Eyni and Sara Khaki, in their feature debut. Follows Sara Shahverdi, the first woman elected as a councilwoman in her Iranian village, as she trains teenage girls to ride motorcycles and fights child marriages. The married filmmaking duo spent seven to eight years filming. Shahverdi is a divorced, motorcycle-riding former midwife. Won the World Cinema Grand Jury Prize: Documentary at Sundance 2025. Made history as the first feature documentary from Iran to earn an Oscar nomination. Rotten Tomatoes: 100% from 28 reviews. Executive produced by Sheila Nevins.

nominees.name: Mr. Nobody Against Putin
details: Directed by David Borenstein and Pasha Talankin. In his hometown in the Ural Mountains, fun-loving Pasha works as a nonconformist elementary school teacher. After Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, his school and community transform from a place of education to one of militarization and state ideology. Students and their family members are recruited to fight. Filmed secretly over two years. When Pasha learns his life may be at risk, he plans a dangerous escape from Russia. Winner of the Sundance World Cinema Documentary Special Jury Award. Rotten Tomatoes: 100% from 39 reviews. Runtime: 90 minutes. A co-production of Denmark, Czechia, and Germany. Acquired by Kino Lorber.

nominees.name: The Alabama Solution (HBO)
details: Directed by Andrew Jarecki and Charlotte Kaufman (the team behind *The Jinx*). Uses footage shot by inmates via contraband cell phones to show violence, forced labor, overcrowding, and crumbling facilities in Alabama's prisons. Also charts the aftermath of Steven Davis's 2019 death — beaten to death by prison guards at Donaldson Correctional Facility. The title comes from Governor Kay Ivey's insistence that Alabama can solve its prison crisis on its own, without federal intervention — her solution: build bigger prisons.

Three main whistleblowers were moved into solitary confinement days before the film received its Oscar nomination; two remain there. Features Melvin Ray, founder of the Free Alabama Movement, responsible for a 23-day statewide prison labor stoppage in 2022. The Shorenstein Center will recognize the film with a Special Citation at the 2026 Goldsmith Awards. Jarecki kept a mnemonic on his whiteboard throughout the six-year production: "SMITWTNOES" — "saddest movie in the world that no one ever sees." Streams on HBO Max. Runtime: approximately 90 minutes.

nominees.name: The Perfect Neighbor (Netflix)
details: Directed by Geeta Gandbhir. Chronicles the June 2, 2023, fatal shooting of Ajike Owens by her white neighbor Susan Louise Lorincz in Ocala, Florida. Told chronologically with limited narration, mostly using pre-existing police body-cam footage. Questions the systemic bias in how Florida's stand-your-ground laws are applied. Gandbhir described the film's body-cam use as intentional subversion: "Police body-cam footage is used to surveil communities of color in order to protect the police, so we wanted to flip that on its head."

Won the Directing Award at Sundance 2025. Surpassed 50 million views on Netflix. Executive produced by Soledad O'Brien. Rotten Tomatoes: 99% from 95 reviews. Runtime: 96 minutes. Gandbhir makes history as the first woman to earn Oscar nominations in both feature and short documentary categories in a single year (also nominated for *The Devil Is Busy*).

nominees.name: Avatar: Fire and Ash — Deborah L. Scott
details: Scott designed costumes for the previous *Avatar* films and won the Oscar for *Titanic*. The third installment introduces the Ash People, a violent Na'vi tribe, requiring new costume silhouettes for the franchise.

nominees.name: Frankenstein — Kate Hawley
details: New Zealand costume designer and longtime del Toro collaborator. Credits include *Pacific Rim*, *Crimson Peak*, and *Suicide Squad*. Her Gothic designs are central to the film's aesthetic.

nominees.name: Hamnet — Malgosia Turzanska
details: Polish-American designer; Zhao's classmate at NYU's Tisch School of the Arts. Credits include *Stranger Things* and *The Green Knight*. Her father passed away during filming, and the experience informed her design of grief: "The movie helped me process my grief, and my grief helped me design the movie." Agnes's opening-scene bodice is made of actual tree bark cloth. Shakespeare's costumes incorporate emotional storytelling — his first doublet has small slashes, but after Hamnet's death they grow larger. Jessie Buckley's now-iconic red dress was inspired by different colors of blood.

nominees.name: Marty Supreme — Miyako Bellizzi
details: A former Vice fashion editor with no formal film training, Bellizzi has been working with the Safdies since *Good Time* (2017) and designed costumes for *Uncut Gems*. A 50-person wardrobe team working around the clock made thousands of costumes, many hand-stitched from scratch. Authenticity was paramount: no contemporary brands anywhere, "even down to the underwear." Gwyneth Paltrow's character features ivory wool coats, pillbox hats, and delicate face veils. A Ken Jacobs short film documenting Jewish immigrant life on Manhattan's Lower East Side in 1955 served as a main reference point.

nominees.name: Sinners — Ruth E. Carter
details: Carter previously won two Oscars for the *Black Panther* films and is one of the most celebrated costume designers working today. The 1930s Jim Crow South setting required extensive period research.

nominees.name: Frankenstein — Mike Hill
details: Hill discussed the concept with del Toro for years. Creating a new look for the Creature was a singular challenge given the hundreds of prior adaptations. Del Toro's emphasis on practical effects made this a standout technical achievement.

nominees.name: Kokuho
details: A 2025 Japanese historical drama directed by Lee Sang-il, based on Shuichi Yoshida's 2018 novel. Stars Ryo Yoshizawa, who trained 18 months in Kabuki theater. Spanning five decades, the film fuses the transcendent beauty of Kabuki with a tale of artistic passion, fame, and rivalry. The elaborate Kabuki stage makeup and aging transformations are central to the nomination. Premiered in Directors' Fortnight at Cannes. Grossed $128 million in Japan, becoming the all-time highest-grossing Japanese live-action film. The first Japanese-produced film nominated for Best Makeup and Hairstyling. Rotten Tomatoes: 93%.

nominees.name: Sinners
details: Period-accurate 1930s styling combined with horror/vampire transformation effects.

nominees.name: The Smashing Machine — A24
details: A 2025 biographical sports drama written and directed by Benny Safdie (Josh's brother, directing solo for the first time). Dwayne Johnson stars as MMA fighter Mark Kerr, with Emily Blunt, Ryan Bader, Bas Rutten, and Oleksandr Usyk. Johnson plays Kerr with heavy prosthetic makeup — a central element of the nomination. Premiered at the 82nd Venice International Film Festival, winning the Silver Lion. Released by A24. Grossed $21 million against a $50 million budget. Rotten Tomatoes: 71%. Score by avant-garde jazz harpist Nala Sinephro.

nominees.name: The Ugly Stepsister
details: A 2025 Norwegian satirical black comedy body horror film, the directorial debut of Emilie Blichfeldt. Using the motif of Cinderella, it tells the story of Elvira competing against her beautiful stepsister in a bloody battle for beauty. Blichfeldt was influenced by the Brothers Grimm's "Aschenputtel" and by David Cronenberg, Dario Argento, Lucio Fulci, and Julia Ducournau's *Raw*. Produced by Maria Ekerhovd of Mer Film — who is also a producer on *Sentimental Value*. Premiered as the opening film of Sundance's Midnight section. Rotten Tomatoes: 96% from 127 reviews.

nominees.name: Frankenstein — Tamara Deverell (Production Design) / Shane Vieau (Set Decoration)
details: Deverell first worked with del Toro on *Mimic* (1997) and later on *The Strain*, *Cabinet of Curiosities*, and *Nightmare Alley*. Victor Frankenstein's laboratory and Captain Anderson's ship were fully constructed sets, per del Toro's insistence on practical environments.

nominees.name: Hamnet — Fiona Crombie (Production Design) / Alice Felton (Set Decoration)
details: Crombie's credits include *Cruella* and *The Favourite*. Over two decades ago, she designed "Hamlet" for Australia's Bell Shakespeare Company — a serendipitous connection to the Shakespeare material. On the Globe Theatre: "It's actually the second Globe, the first burnt down, so we had creative freedom to make a Globe that felt appropriate for our story." Collaborated closely with cinematographer Łukasz Żal.

nominees.name: Marty Supreme — Jack Fisk (Production Design)
details: Arguably the greatest living film production designer, Fisk turned 80 during the production. His credits include *Badlands*, *Carrie*, *Days of Heaven*, *Mulholland Drive*, *There Will Be Blood*, *The Revenant*, and *Killers of the Flower Moon*. This is his fourth Oscar nomination and first Safdie collaboration. For the table tennis parlor, Fisk recreated Lawrence's Broadway Table Tennis Club, the first Black-owned business in that area of New York. The building no longer exists, but Fisk had access to blueprints and Time Life photographs. Safdie was obsessed with accuracy, even searching for the right size ping pong balls — they were slightly smaller in the 1950s.

nominees.name: One Battle After Another — Florencia Martin (Production Design) / Anthony Carlino (Set Decoration)
Sinners — Hannah Beachler (Production Design) / Monique Champagne (Set Decoration)
details: Beachler won the Oscar for *Black Panther* (2019), becoming the first African American to win in this category.

nominees.name: Frankenstein
details: Gothic horror sound design supporting del Toro's practical-effects-driven vision.

nominees.name: F1
details: Capturing the sonic experience of Formula 1 racing at immersive volume. Real Grand Prix footage integrated with cinematic sequences.

nominees.name: One Battle After Another
details: Action-heavy car chases and shootouts required elaborate sound work for Anderson's most expensive and action-oriented film.

nominees.name: Sinners
details: The interplay between diegetic blues music and horror sound design was widely praised. Music is central to the story and the sound mix balances Ludwig Göransson's score, live blues performances, and vampire-horror elements.

nominees.name: Sirât
details: Oliver Laxe's contemplative desert road film features a distinctive sonic landscape, with electronic musician Kangding Ray's score layered over naturalistic desert ambience. Also nominated for Best International Feature.

nominees.name: F1 — Stephen Mirrione, ACE
details: Mirrione won the Oscar for *Traffic* (2001). He worked with Kosinski on *Top Gun: Maverick* and *Spiderhead*.

nominees.name: Marty Supreme — Ronald Bronstein & Josh Safdie
details: The duo's signature frenetic editing style. Bronstein also co-wrote the screenplay.

nominees.name: One Battle After Another — Andy Jurgensen
details: Jurgensen has worked with Anderson since *Inherent Vice* (2014). The nearly three-hour film is noted for its relentless pacing.

nominees.name: Sentimental Value — Olivier Bugge Coutté
details: Coutté is Joachim Trier's longtime editor.

nominees.name: Sinners — Michael P. Shawver
details: Shawver has edited all of Coogler's films.

nominees.name: Frankenstein — Dan Laustsen
details: Laustsen was nominated for *The Shape of Water* and *Nightmare Alley*, both for del Toro. Their collaboration stretches back to *Mimic* (1997).

nominees.name: Marty Supreme — Darius Khondji
details: Khondji shot on 35mm using Arriflex cameras and vintage Panavision C Series and B Series anamorphic lenses to recreate the 1950s. Known for work with David Fincher (*Se7en*) and Michael Haneke. Previously nominated for *Bardo* and *Evita*.

nominees.name: One Battle After Another — Michael Bauman
details: Shot using VistaVision, becoming one of the first films to use the format since the 1960s.

nominees.name: Sinners — Autumn Durald Arkapaw
details: Combined Ultra Panavision 70 and IMAX formats. This is her first Oscar nomination.

nominees.name: Train Dreams — Adolpho Veloso
details: Shot entirely in natural light using an ARRI Alexa 35. Veloso built a customized candle rig to work around the set's no-open-flames rule. His cinematography captures the expansive American West.

nominees.name: Avatar: Fire and Ash
details: James Cameron's third *Avatar* installment, pushing boundaries of motion capture and CGI. Budget: $350 million, plus approximately $150 million in marketing. Opened to $345 million globally. Crossed $1 billion worldwide. IMAX revenue surged 35% in Q4 2025, with the film contributing $112 million in IMAX ticket sales. Stars Sam Worthington, Zoe Saldaña, Sigourney Weaver, Stephen Lang, and Oona Chaplin as Varang. Runtime: 3 hours 15 minutes. Rotten Tomatoes: 66% — the lowest-rated film nominated in Picture-adjacent technical categories. Cameron indicated the final two sequels may not proceed if the film is unsuccessful.

nominees.name: F1
details: VFX supervisor Ryan Tudhope and Framestore leveraged techniques from *Top Gun: Maverick* to re-skin real Grand Prix cars with fictional APXGP cars.

nominees.name: Jurassic World Rebirth
details: Directed by Gareth Edwards, written by David Koepp (who co-wrote the original *Jurassic Park*). Stars Scarlett Johansson, Mahershala Ali, and Jonathan Bailey. Contains 1,515 VFX shots — the most in the franchise. ILM completed over 1,000. Budget: $180–225 million. Gross: over $869 million worldwide. Scored by Alexandre Desplat, recorded at Abbey Road Studios with a 105-piece orchestra and 60-piece choir; Jonathan Bailey performed the clarinet solo. Jurassic World Rebirth debuted to a 58% score on Rotten Tomatoes — making it the lowest-rated of all Best Visual Effects nominees this year. The first franchise entry nominated for an Oscar since *The Lost World*. Rotten Tomatoes score noted.

nominees.name: Sinners
details: Vampire transformation sequences and period-horror effects blended with practical work.

nominees.name: The Lost Bus
details: Directed by Paul Greengrass, co-written with Brad Ingelsby. Based on Lizzie Johnson's 2021 book *Paradise* about the 2018 Camp Fire. Stars Matthew McConaughey and America Ferrera. At the heart is the real-life story of school-bus driver Kevin McKay and teacher Mary Ludwig, who rescued 22 children. VFX handled by beloFX, Cinesite, ILM, and RISE. Exterior sequences filmed by DOP Pål Ulvik Rokseth in Ruidoso, New Mexico. Editor William Goldenberg noted the film took on extra resonance while working on post-production in London as his L.A. home was evacuated during the 2025 Pacific Palisades fire. Released on Apple TV+. Rotten Tomatoes: 87%.

nominees.name: A Friend of Dorothy
details: Directed by Lee Knight. Miriam Margolyes stars as Dorothy and Alistair Nwachukwu plays JJ. An intergenerational tale about an unlikely friendship between an elderly woman and her teenage neighbor. The title is a throwback to Judy Garland's Dorothy in *The Wizard of Oz* — "a friend of Dorothy" was once slang for gay men. Inspired by a real-life friendship between Knight, his husband, and their late neighbor. Knight wrote the script specifically for the 84-year-old Margolyes (best known as Professor Sprout in the Harry Potter films), five years before production. Also features Stephen Fry. Won at the British Independent Film Awards. Runtime: 21 minutes.

nominees.name: Butcher's Stain
details: The debut professional film of writer-director Meyer Levinson-Blount, a student at Tel Aviv University's Steve Tisch School of Film and Television. Samir (Omar Sameer Mahamid), an Arab Israeli working in a Tel Aviv supermarket, is accused of tearing down hostage posters. It is eventually revealed the posters are simply falling off the wall on their own. Levinson-Blount cast himself as the accuser: "I didn't want to make a film about racism and discrimination and act like I've cleansed myself entirely of any sense of responsibility." Won a Silver Medal at the 2025 Student Academy Awards. Israel's culture minister Miki Zohar criticized the film. Runtime: 26 minutes.

nominees.name: Jane Austen's Period Drama
details: Co-written and directed by Julia Aks and Steve Pinder. In 1813 England, Miss Estrogenia Talbot's marriage proposal is interrupted when she gets her period. Initially a three-minute sketch, the filmmakers expanded it after Aks solicited personal menstruation stories from a private Facebook group for female opera singers. Emma Thompson joined as "Executive Menstrual Advisor," declining a traditional producer credit. Much of the comedic male ignorance was lifted from real tweets posted in 2022. Won the Comedy Jury Award at Aspen Shortsfest. A feature-length version is in development. Runtime: 13 minutes.

nominees.name: The Singers
details: Directed by Sam A. Davis. Based on Ivan Turgenev's story from *A Sportsman's Sketches* (1852), discovered by Davis via George Saunders' 2021 book *A Swim in a Pond in the Rain*. Depicts an improvised sing-off by downtrodden patrons in a pub. Shot on 35mm with music recorded live on set. Davis spent a year scouring social media for unsung talent. The cast includes Mike Young (film debut), Australia's *The Voice* winner Judah Kelly, New Orleans busker Will Harrington, operatic tenor Matthew Corcoran, and folk-blues legend Chris Smither. Won 35 awards across 50 festivals. Netflix acquired global streaming rights. Runtime: 18 minutes.

nominees.name: Two People Exchanging Saliva
details: Written and directed by Natalie Musteata and Alexandre Singh. In a dystopian world, kissing is a crime punishable by death. Malaise, a young woman hired at an upscale department store, earns the interest of a wealthy housewife. Stars Zar Amir (*Holy Spider*), Luàna Bajrami (*Portrait of a Lady on Fire*), with the voice of Vicky Krieps (*Phantom Thread*). Executive produced by Isabelle Huppert and Julianne Moore. Shot in black-and-white largely at the Galeries Lafayette in Paris. Inspired by a couple in Tehran arrested and sentenced to 10 years for dancing in the streets. Premiered at Telluride in a program curated by Barry Jenkins. Won the Grand Jury Prize at AFI Fest. Shortlisted for the 2026 César Awards. Runtime: 36 minutes — the longest live action short nominee.

nominees.name: Butterfly
details: Directed by Florence Miailhe. Produced by Ron Dyens (Sacrebleu Productions). The striking, painterly film condenses the life of Alfred Nakache, a Jewish Olympic swimmer who competed at the 1936 Berlin Games, survived Auschwitz, and returned to swim again. Miailhe has honed a distinctive "film-painting" style using oil, pastel, and sand directly under the camera. She had a personal connection: she took swimming lessons with Alfred's brother William, and her father knew Alfred from the French Resistance. Nakache's wife and daughter died at Auschwitz. Almost no dialogue. Won Best French Short Film at Annecy 2024 and the Generation Kplus Best Short Film at Berlin. Producer Dyens shared in last year's Best Animated Feature win for *Flow*. Runtime: 15 minutes.

nominees.name: Forevergreen
details: Directed by Nathan Engelhardt and Jeremy Spears, Disney animation department graduates with credits on *Zootopia* and *Encanto*. A dialogue-free environmental fable about a bear cub who forms a bond with a sentient evergreen tree. Over 200 industry veteran volunteers collaborated over five years. The team invented a new animation technique, the "wobble," combining CG with a carved-wood look. Features bluegrass music (banjo, mandolin, acoustic guitar). Ends with a Bible quote. Runtime: 13 minutes.

nominees.name: Retirement Plan
details: Directed by John Kelly (debut film), co-written with Tara Lawall. An Irish animated short following Ray (voiced by Domhnall Gleeson), a middle-aged man overwhelmed by life who dreams about retirement. Kelly described it as "basically a panic attack made into a short film." Animation style is intentionally minimalist, citing Roy Andersson, Wim Wenders, and Jean-Pierre Melville. Won the Grand Jury Award and Audience Award at SXSW 2025 and Best of the Festival at Palm Springs ShortFest. Gleeson came on as executive producer. Commissioned by Screen Ireland and RTÉ. Variety predicts it will win the Oscar. Runtime: 7 minutes — the shortest of all nominees across all short categories.

nominees.name: The Girl Who Cried Pearls
details: Directed by Chris Lavis and Maciek Szczerbowski. A stop-motion film about a young boy who falls in love with a girl who secretly weeps pearls at night. Produced by the National Film Board of Canada (NFB), which has earned 11 Oscars and 79 nominations. Inspired during production of their 2007 Oscar-nominated short *Madame Tutli-Putli*, when a prop necklace fell apart, scattering pearls. The directors used recycled street waste to build miniature sets. Five years to complete. Features narrator Colm Feore and music by Patrick Watson. Won Best Canadian Short Film at TIFF. Premiered at Annecy 2025. Runtime: 17 minutes.

nominees.name: The Three Sisters
details: Directed by Konstantin Bronzit under the pseudonym "Timur Kognov" — a fictional identity with an invented biography (born 1969 in Georgia, living in Toronto). Bronzit revealed his authorship after the Oscar shortlist announcement, calling it a "social experiment" to test whether the film could succeed without his established name. Three sisters on a remote island are disrupted by a gregarious sailor tenant. Sharply drawn 2D animation with no dialogue — only seagull cries and the sailor's grin. Bronzit worked on the film for six years, inspired by a Montenegrin legend from the town of Prčanj. This is his third Oscar nomination (after *Lavatory – Lovestory* and *We Can't Live Without Cosmos*). Runtime: 14 minutes. Israel/Cyprus co-production.

nominees.name: All the Empty Rooms
details: Directed by Joshua Seftel. Produced by Conall Jones. Features CBS News reporter Steve Hartman, who wrote a letter to every family who had lost a child to gun violence since Sandy Hook, asking to visit their home to photograph their child's preserved bedroom. Each room looks exactly as it did on each child's final day. Hartman explained that the sheer volume of school shootings and growing numbness drove the project. Seftel and Hartman share a 25-year professional history. Produced by Adam McKay's Hyperobject Industries. This is Seftel's second Oscar nomination (after *Stranger at the Gate*, 2022). Debuted at Telluride. The predicted frontrunner. Streams on Netflix. Runtime: 33–35 minutes.

nominees.name: Armed Only with a Camera: The Life and Death of Brent Renaud
details: Directed by Brent Renaud and Craig Renaud. Chronicles the years the Renaud brothers spent covering the world's most dangerous conflicts. Brent became the first Western journalist killed by Russian soldiers in Ukraine on March 13, 2022, at age 50. The film includes footage Brent filmed on the day he was killed. Co-produced by Colombian photojournalist Juan Arredondo, who was with Brent that day. Includes frank discussions of Brent's autism. Chronicles Brent's evolution from his early years with Jon Alpert at DCTV. The Brent Renaud Foundation has been established to continue his legacy. A narrative feature adaptation has been announced. Released by HBO Documentary Films. Runtime: 37 minutes.

nominees.name: Children No More: "Were and Are Gone"
details: Directed by Hilla Medalia. An observational documentary about a vigil that began in March 2025, when women stood silently in a Tel Aviv public square holding photographs of children killed in Gaza — each image bearing the child's name, age, date of death, and the words "WAS AND IS NO MORE." Produced by Medalia, Yael Melamede, and documentary legend Sheila Nevins (winner of over 30 Primetime Emmys) — Nevins' second Oscar nomination in as many years. No U.S. distributor would take the film; Nevins commented: "It was like they didn't want to anger anybody. This film angers people." Israeli culture minister Miki Zohar lambasted the film. Made entirely without government funding. Runtime: 36 minutes.

nominees.name: The Devil Is Busy
details: Co-directed by Christalyn Hampton and Geeta Gandbhir. In a post-Roe landscape, Tracii, head of security at an abortion clinic, begins her day in prayer, shielding patients and staff from protesters and violence. The film documents staff being forced to turn away patients who missed restrictive cutoffs by just one day. Released by HBO Documentary Films. Gandbhir is also nominated for Best Documentary Feature for *The Perfect Neighbor*, making her the first woman to earn Oscar nominations in both documentary categories in a single year. Runtime: 31 minutes.

nominees.name: Perfectly a Strangeness
details: Directed by Alison McAlpine. Three donkeys discover an abandoned astronomical observatory in Chile's Atacama Desert and gaze into the cosmos. A meditative, sensory exploration of what nonfiction cinema can be. No dialogue. Debuted at the 2024 Cannes Film Festival. Won Best Documentary Short at the Chicago International Film Festival, the Grand Prize at Montreal's Festival du Nouveau Cinema, and Best Short at Full Frame (qualifying it for the Oscars). Runtime: 15 minutes — the shortest documentary short nominee. Canada/Chile co-production. Available on Vimeo On Demand, Pleins Écrans, and Crave.

categories.name: BEST CASTING (Inaugural Category)

nominees.name: Hamnet — Nina Gold
details: One of the most acclaimed casting directors working today: *Game of Thrones*, *Star Wars: The Force Awakens*, *The King's Speech*, and *The Crown*. She got her start as an extra in an AC/DC music video. For *Hamnet*, she recalled choosing Paul Mescal and Jessie Buckley: "I've been looking at Jessie, actually, since before she was a professional actress." Her biggest worry was casting the children: "I used to wake up in the middle of the night thinking, we're not going to find them." She found brothers Jacobi and Noah Jupe to play Hamnet and Hamlet.

nominees.name: Marty Supreme — Jennifer Venditti
details: Specializes in "street casting," finding talent in unexpected places. Previously cast *Uncut Gems* and other Safdie projects. She brought in Abel Ferrara, found *Shark Tank* regular Kevin O'Leary a major role, cast Tyler, the Creator, and discovered Luke Manley, who had never acted before. "I started as an insatiably curious little girl who was obsessed with people-watching."

nominees.name: One Battle After Another — Cassandra Kulukundis
details: Has worked on all 10 of Anderson's films, from *Hard Eight* (1996) through *One Battle After Another*. Six of the films she cast for Anderson were nominated for Best Picture. Her work on this film unlocked four Oscar-nominated performances (DiCaprio, del Toro, Penn, Taylor). On the six-year search for Chase Infiniti, she recalls receiving screenshots of people's TVs from friends. When she saw Infiniti in a K-pop dancing group: "She was in school and hadn't done much, but I got her pages, and she was graceful, yet strong."

nominees.name: The Secret Agent — Gabriel Domingues
details: Cast Brazilian actors and non-actors, seeking authentic Northeastern accents for the 1970s Recife setting. Also a screenwriter. The sole nominee from a non-English language film: "Maybe they don't look like what people usually expect to see in a movie, but they are actors."

nominees.name: Sinners — Francine Maisler
details: One of Hollywood's most prolific casting directors, working with Denis Villeneuve, Alejandro G. Iñárritu, Greta Gerwig, Barry Jenkins, Michael Mann, and Coogler. *Sinners* won outstanding ensemble performance at the SAG Awards. Maisler found Miles Caton, who nearly earned a Supporting Actor nomination himself. "Putting Sinners together was one of the greatest experiences of my life, because I entered the world of blues, which I wasn't familiar with." She is in pole position to win the inaugural award.

notes: On February 8, 2024, the Academy announced the creation of this category: "Casting directors play an essential role in filmmaking, and as the Academy evolves, we are proud to add casting to the disciplines that we recognize and celebrate." A category for casting had previously been rejected in 1999.

footer_note: *This document reflects the extraordinary breadth and diversity of the 98th Academy Awards field — from Ryan Coogler's record-breaking horror epic and Paul Thomas Anderson's sprawling political thriller, to intimate Scandinavian family dramas, a secret Iranian Palme d'Or winner, literary adaptations, animation phenomena, and documentary work of profound urgency, all recognized across 24 categories including the historic debut of Best Casting.*
